speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 5 of the interview with Yvette Lennon on February 12, 1995. You were saying that you brought in--

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, where's your sister? [BACKROUND NOISE] I  show her. And she says-- I'll-- she says-- she told the block leaders, you have to give her two soups a day. This is my order. Then she says, and, and don't let her outside. And she got the orders then. I-- and she says, and you, you're going to work in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there, but the barrels [BACKROUND NOISE] were so heavy to carry, I couldn't even-- I was-- I couldn't carry them. And I told-- then when I met her-- oh, she took me to the office. And she says, wait here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says to the girl, give me  a loaf of bread. I want to give to someone. And she gave me the loaf of  bread. And I brought it at, at the block. And then she says to me-- after three days she saw me. I  say, I can't work. It's so hard, that work. I can't lift the barrels. She was laughing then. She says, all right, you better clean up. You're going to clean up the block where they sleep, the girls, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every morning, I would get up at 4:00 in the morning like I was supposed to. And I went to the block. And I made all the beds. And then I swept the block.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took all the dead people who were in the bed-- because they were all dying. If they were on the bed, I had to get them off the bed. And I closed my eyes. And I would put them-- like, you'd put the garbage in the corner on one side, and on this side, I put the bodies. And then I picked them up and throw them-- and I did this every day. And then when I took my, my sister-- and we had the Greek girl who also broke her finger. And they did nothing for her. She was in terrible pain. So there were both, like, not good enough to do anything. So I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, like, we were the three of us. I was the one who  was trying to do the things. So-- and then I would go back to the block. They give me an extra soup for this. So I  come to the block. And I see-- and when the girls came from the kitchen, I would run back to the block, and I would change. That's what they did there.

speaker: interviewee
text: They say, you, you, you organize. He says-- it means that if I give them the soup, they give me something from the kitchen, like butter. And that-- I said so that I could give my sister. She needs vitamins, she says to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I gave them-- they would give me a piece of butter, of some cheese, of some whatever. Or especially flour-- my sister asks-- says, ask for flour. So I would take the flour.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I'll go back to the block, running. And I will-- my sister and the other girl, Julie, they would make the flour. They will make it-- there was a little stove there. And they would make little pancakes. And they would put them on the little stove. And then just water, you know. And then I'll go back to the block, change the pancakes for something else. And this is  what I was doing every day. And in the meantime, so far, we were all right. And everybody was dying in the block. They were all-- the bathroom there, it was like a river coming down. They were all sick. And in the meantime-- and then, one of the days, it was coming close-- and we-- then we saw all the-- all the camps were leaving. They were evacuating them because they wanted-- the Germans were coming in. But they didn't know-- so our camp wasn't yet evacuated. And then I said to my sister, who was getting a bit better-- she was feeling better. And I was doing OK. As long-- you know, I didn't have food much, but I was feeling all right. So in the meantime, I said-- I said to my sister-- says to me, they're, they're leaving. Everybody's leaving. But then, I saw suddenly-- we were very fortunate. My sister says to me, listen, they're all leaving. You better leave with them, she says, because they're going to kill me. We says to her, if she can't walk, they shoot you, she says, and you leave. Because I knew our camp will be next.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, no, I'm not gonna leave. I'm not gonna leave you here to die alone. I-- she says, what are you gonna do? She says, you want-- if you die-- why should you die?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no way. I'm not leaving here. And then, when I went to bed, I couldn't sleep that night. I, I was saying my prayers. I was saying something, please, something should happen. I don't want-- I, I would-- never would leave her. And it happened. The Germans came-- the Nazis came in three days before by surprise. The, the, the British came three days before. They didn't expect the British. So they were going to take our camp, too. They knew how, how close they were. But they took them by surprise. They came three days before. And they caught the Nazis then and that, and we didn't have to leave. So I say, see, now we're not going to  leave. We are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the British took over. And the Nazis-- so-- and I was-- what I was doing-- something terrible happened to me. See, I saved her life. If I wasn't there, my sister-- she always did things for me. But I saved her life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now she saved my life. I was doing fine up to that day. I worked hard, and I [? decide-- ?] so happy that we were gonna to leave, so happy that [INAUDIBLE] were last. And we made it. And I was hungry. And I found a piece of  bread somewhere on the-- on top of the closet somewhere. And I ate another piece of bread-- piece of cheese. This cheese was bad cheese. And I got what they all got it. And I, I  was dying. I-- you know, I had to go to that place-- the bathroom-- I couldn't even walk--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was-- what did they call it? Dysentery.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's something that makes you eat because they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --not eating.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was making them sick. I was so sick. Three time days, I was back and forth running up to that bathroom. And, and another Greek girl, too, was running with me. And then, then third day, I saw how she-- dead on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my sister saw me, that all the energy that I wanted to leave was gone. I didn't have energy now to survive anymore, not that I didn't want to. How much energy are you going to use in your body when this came, and there was no food in your stomach?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- my sister went out of her mind. She was-- what she did-- the British had  come in. They were so confused at what they saw. They then came in. They gave other people to give us the food. They didn't know where to go, where to start first with what they  saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they brought us little cans of food inside. They started giving it to the block leaders and give them-- before, they didn't come into the block because they had a lot of other work to do. And inside the cans, there were different things. In one can, it was, like, dry coffee, you know. And so you put water, and you make it, you know. She, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she-- my sister, who was so smart, she said, what am I going to do? So she took a piece-- a little of the coffee-- she put in one teaspoon coffee. And then she took a piece of wood. She made the charcoal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she put it next to the-- to the stove. And she, she made it like charcoal. And she put a little of the coffee in there. And she says to me-- she says to me-- I wasn't even aware of what was going on. She says, open your mouth. So I didn't-- not because I didn't want to. She says, open your mouth, or I'll kill you. She says, you're not going to leave me alone in this  world. She says, you wanted me to leave. Now you are going to leave. And she starts screaming at me. The girl say, she was going to kill you. She says, I'll kill her if she doesn't open her mouth. But she twisted my arm so bad, I felt it. And I opened my mouth-- [? because ?] scream. And she shoved this, this spoon in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you believe it? If here, you go to doctors, then you-- they take you for days and days until you get better. She, she did that. And then it work out that-- of course I kept going, but not like before, to that bathroom. For three days, I was, like, not all myself, but I was getting better-- a little bit better, a little bit better until I got out of it by the end of the week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then came something better yet. Two British doctors walked in, just when I was feeling better. I start-- no, my sister told me, go and get-- she says, now that you're feeling better, everybody's wearing clothes. Let's get all these filthy things we have. You go and find-- my sister couldn't walk. The other one had the finger. She says, but I don't have the strength. She says, slowly. Walk slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went, and I found so many clothes. And I laid down sleeping, you know. And then I pick up three things for my sister. And we got dressed. We got washed, and that and that, and looking more like a human being. And now these doctors walk in. And they say, who speaks English here? There they came-- only me, he said, because they spoke German. See, I've got everywhere I found because of music, or that. I say, you speak-- can you help us, please? So I went with them, and I told them in the bed. And I  spoke German because of listening. I learn languages easy back-- by ear. That I was-- I didn't speak like they speak, but I knew how to talk. So I was telling the girls, what do you feel, and when-- I tell the doctor. And they came back the next day, and the third day again. And the second day-- the third day, the doctor comes. And he  says to me, we're not going to work here anymore. They are going to finish with the blocks. We are going to work at the hospital. So we would like to have you there. Would you work for us? And the hospital there, they will tell you, a small hospital that-- built for the momentarily. They had it there. So I said, sure, I will work. She said, all right then. Let's go find some clothing, some good clothing. And then, here is a card.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- I'll show you what you-- where you're gonna live. She was in one of these little beautiful houses. She brought me there. And he says, I want by Monday, everything cleaned here, [INAUDIBLE], beds, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, here we're gonna live? I said, what about my sister and your-- my cousin [NON-ENGLISH]? They're gonna live with you. Don't worry. So it's closer to the hospital. He got me out of this mess because it took the time for the people to get out. So I say, you mean-- and then he said, let's go and get some nice, clean clothes and that, [? Hebrew. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he says, no offense. He says, you're going to go to the sauna, disinfect. Because we had lice sometimes because of what-- there was lice there. That's why they were all getting sick. We did all that by the end of the week. And he gave me a card. We are going to eat at the canteen with the British soldiers every day. He says, I'll give you a few days. Settle down. And then when we went-- when we went to the canteen, they gave us the food. And the sugar was a full bowl of sugar. We ate the whole sugar. And the next day they didn't-- they put only enough for the tea. [LAUGHTER] We were so hungry, you know. And then we-- to see food, real food. And the doctor was very, very nice. There was two doctors of them. So I worked for them at the hospital. And then, suddenly as I was going to work, the other one said he wasn't there. And he said-- he said, you're-- you're not coming anymore because this-- we're going to burn Belsen. It's gonna get burned, the whole camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, well, where are we going? He says, I don't know. And-- they don't tell you anything till the last minute. So the other doctor wasn't there. And I wanted to say goodbye to him and thank him for everything he has done for me. He says it's his day off. I say-- I say, please tell him how much-- thank him for this. So-- and then I saw a motor pool. We were all on line. I come home, my sister says we're leaving. I say, where are we going? She says, we're going to a hotels in beautiful place. And then Belsen, they're going to put us-- I think we leave home. So we're standing all in line with our things. And suddenly I see that doctor. He says, don't you want to work for us anymore? I said, yes, but the doctor says we're leaving.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I know where you're going to go. He says, we are going to Warsaw close by, only 15 minutes. They opened up a big hospital in Himmler's villa. They took that. And they made it a tremendous hospital. And he says, I'll, I'll pick you-- I say, I'll send you somebody to pick you up to see where we are. And I went, and I worked for them there. He was-- and then we started to feel better, to look better. You know, like we were eating better because two months had passed, and that. And, and there was a dance going on in the-- for the British sergeants and, and British-- for sergeants and for, for the doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he told me if I would go with him to the dance. I said, me, with you? I couldn't believe because I was just a little skeleton there, you know. Now he's telling me to go to the  dance. But this had changed, you know. You, you get better.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and then he left. They left. They brought Belgian doctors, and I didn't work anymore. Now we were looking for my brother and the survivors. And we couldn't-- he wasn't in the list. And on the other hand, my brother was alive. But he didn't know-- he thought we were dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there another three months. And then suddenly, my brother was liberated by the Russians. And they didn't take good care of him. He was dying. And it's not like the British.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the little girls-- there were a few girls. They took care of him. And they all went to Bamberg, Germany. They traveled with the train. They went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he looked in the list. He didn't find us. So at the end, he thought we were dead. And we-- my sister-- my sister was saying, Michele is dead. Michele is dead. And they're saying, no he's not dead. I say-- I say, you see, he's not dead, I was telling her. She says, why is he not in the list? I say, I don't know. So anyway, we go there. One day, my brother-- one of-- I worked in the Polish camp. There was a Greek camp, too. But we slept in the Polish camp because I was working there. So a Greek girl came to visit us in the Polish camp. And she, she says-- Yvette, she says, you know what? I've got a letter from your brother. Somebody sent a letter. I said, where is it? He's alive? I say, well, somebody gave me a letter to give it to you. I said, I'll bring it to you. I say, where's the letter? She says, no, that person want you to come there to my camp. We went over. We got dressed. My sister and me were running with them. We went, a  half an hour walk. And who was there? My brother, next to-- behind the door. He, he says-- when he arrived closer, he started to get nervous. He says, where is the Greek girls? Where is the Greek girls? They was travelling for three days. And then he came with all the little friends that he had. They all came together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then a girl once came to our camp, one of the Polish girls who came to see all these Polish girls. She says, Michele, I saw a  girl walking in the street. She looks like you. She says, she probably could be your sister. But she didn't tell me nothing, the girl. Soon my brother is when he-- they all decide to come to the camp. And it was. And, and so that day, or some day, you know, we were all so excited.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in Bergen-Belsen with him for a few days, five days. And then we all took the train, and we went to Munich. And that is where they were going to send us back to Greece, in Munich. So we waited there about three weeks. And one month in Munich. And then they-- the plane came. And then we all-- we became like a family. Because after-- I'll show you that picture that we're all together. This is the ones and-- we came back to Greece with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we got into-- in four, five hours we were in Athens. And then we went in Athens-- they took us to a special place, you know, to stay for two weeks. They let us stay in some kind of a place, hotels, whatever. And from Athens, we took the bus to Salonika. And we tried to find a job. I mean, we went to-- we didn't have a-- our house, there was these Greek people living there. We took a room someplace. But the one who cut her finger, she lost all her family. So we thought that we're living-- we're going to live together. And there was three young men, too. We became like a family. And-- so we shared-- so my sister went to the place where we used to live. And she says to the woman, we lived here. This is our  house. She says, well, we live here now. She says, look, we're going to live together. So we shared the house with her and her husband. And we all lived there. And we all went-- moved there. And my brother went to look for a job. And he says, you are girls. I don't like girls in Greece working in cafes. I'll, I'll put you to someplace else, maybe at the British sergeants' club. They are more  serious, you know, there. And he says, I have two sisters. I play the bass. My sister plays the accordion, and my brother the piano. And another from the concentration camp played the violin-- another young man. And then we had two Greeks-- Greek men. One played the drums and the other played the saxophone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we did a good job there. We were playing for the British sergeants. And, and my husband happened to be one of the sergeants. I married him. You see what happened-- because I-- my brother said, you don't talk to sergeants or to anybody. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I understand. I say-- he says-- and then my husband used to come-- they, they, they were stationed out a half an  hour away. But they used to bring them to have a good time. They had to-- they had girls dancing with them, and they had good food. And then they have games upstairs. And it faced the ocean. It was so beautiful there in Salonika at least. And then he says to me, you know, to talk to the-- but one day, he talked to me. And I thought, he'll kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came in the balcony. And my sister said to him, do you see who's she's talking to? To a sergeant. He says, oh, Jamie. Oh, that's OK, he said. He liked him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and he-- and he said he wanted to marry me. And he asked me on a date. But I couldn't get out because my brother would never give-- seven days a week we  worked. I asked the Greek woman. She says, I'll tell your brother that you go swimming. And then burn-- in the sun-- in the sun you're gonna get burned. And I'll tell, tell her that you are having a sunstroke and that you can't go to work, she says. And you go and meet him. And then you better be here at 10:30, or I'll kill you, she says, before your sister and brother come back. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long-- how long did you know your husband before he  asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know him. I, I-- he came to the balcony. He talked to us, me and my sister. And he's not Jewish. And I told him-- one day, I say, wait till I tell him I'm Jewish. He won't talk to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to him, don't you hate the Jews? Why you talking to me for? Don't you hate the Jews like the rest of the world? He says, no, I don't. Why should I? I said, we had Jewish neighbors in my country, and we love them. So he says-- he talked about God. That's what pleased me. I say, oh, he must be a good person.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said yes. When, when I get married, he says, I don't care where the girl comes from, as long as she's a  good girl. He told me-- it just so happens, by chance, that my husband-- they would-- usually, they play games. He didn't dance. They played games upstairs. That day, they didn't have-- they drink a little bit. They didn't have drinks. And they came downstairs. That's when I came in to study my double bass. And nobody was there but the waitresses. And he was there with a group of young men. And then he told me, when I saw you coming, I  asked them, who am I. He says, this is the girl I'm going to get married to. He said-- I said-- I want to see her character, what kind of a character she has. And that's when he tried to talk, you know, to start talking. And then he asked again-- he asked my brother. I went on that date-- he asked to marry me. And then, then we told my brother and sister. And they said they wanted to see me happy. But I  have to go to England. It means I have to be separated after I was with them together. But she says, no, no, we don't want to lose your chance. He says-- I say-- we want you to be happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to England. We lived five years in England. And then my brother came to the United States first because he sent one of his compositions that he, he won-- he got-- he got an award for, for the best composer here. And he's going to give it to the-- that, that composition to the museum.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wrote it, It's all about the Holocaust. And he got an award for the best composer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he is-- so and they-- he studied here for two years. He got scholarship. And then my sister-- he brought my sister, left my sister with the neighbors. Then he brought my sister here. And she started to teach the piano.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a  year later, they asked me-- that was five years later-- if we want to come back with us to be here with them. And [? my heart ?] says yes, we want. Because in England it was very difficult to get a house, to get everything, with the food, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and your husband  was American or British?

speaker: interviewee
text: British.

speaker: interviewer
text: British.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And so my brother says, why don't you all come here? And my husband says, yeah, I want to  go to the United States. So-- and then we all reunited ourselves here. And he got a job, my husband, here in the place-- he used to work for Kodak in England. And they transferred-- no, they transfer him. He worked for photography again. I mean, in the office, not, not just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not taking pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: --pictures. But he loves it. Because he is used to taking pictures, a lot of pictures, yes. And, and then we're all here, in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's some story.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son became a, a-- my son is-- goes on tour for the-- with the-- to Japan and East Asia. He plays the viola. And he's, he's joined the New York Symphonic Ensemble, with members of the New York Philharmonic and the Metropolitan Opera. That's part of the tour. Then he goes to East Asia. Then he goes-- he plays three months on tour. He's now on tour. That's why he's not here. With the New York City Opera-- he plays. And he lived in, in Manhattan. But now he moved to St. Paul. He wants to get into the-- probably he'd like to be in the Chamber Ensemble from Saint Paul. But, you know, that's a very nice, well-known orchestra.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and my daughter got married. And she lives not far from, from us, you know. And she had the three kids. And this is us. But this is some story, eh? Quite a busy life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And now everything's like normal. So because, because all we had before-- some story-- like, when you start thinking of it, how did you make all this? You know, how do you make out? Because we were sick. I had-- I had the sicknesses there. I had, like I told you, I didn't eat. And then when I came out, that's the worst part. That's important. When I came out, I was feeling great, great. I was-- I worked a year in, in Greece and was-- then I went-- before, when I went to England, everything started coming-- showing the effects of the camp. I started to have-- my nerves in my system-- not in my head, in my system were weakened. And I didn't know that nerves had a-- give you a problem. I thought nerves were when you're mentally sick. But when my body started to feel-- I couldn't breathe. In England, I was-- I went to work. And I was looking around. And I say-- I couldn't get my breath out. And for a month I was choking-- for a year. And then at last, I decided to go to the doctor next door. There was a Jewish doctor. And I went in.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was so bad. I throw everything I was doing, and I go there. And I say-- there were so many people there. She says, who are you? the nurse. I say-- I say, I want to see the doctor right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's-- but-- and the doctor heard me crying. He says, come on in. And I say-- and she-- and he saw-- and I saw my number in my arm. He says-- she says, your heart is fine, she says. You're not-- you're choking because your nerves are weak. The ones around the heart, they're tightening your heart. And that's why you can't breathe well. But don't get scared. So she gave medicine. But it's uncomfortable. Five years in England, I was fighting for breath. And yes, I was keep on going. I got a job. And, and also, I got-- all the skin of my hands peeled off, just like that. And I was in agony with the pain. They send me to-- I went to-- they-- from-- they sent me to so many doctors-- that doctor, he sent me from the skin doctor. And at the  end he says, send the patient to the hospital. So I went, not to stay. I went to the specialist there in the hospital. He, he gave me pills for nerves. He says that the nerves make your skin like that, that that will hurt you, you know. So he-- so he gave me medicine for that, too. And the five years in England was tough. I couldn't-- I wasn't feeling right. But I was keep, keep-- we don't-- because we didn't have enough-- the rooms were not the greatest rooms. We don't-- everything was wrong, the places we were living, you know. You have to put up and pay your own money. And stay-- and so you eat less because they charge you so much for a furnished apartment or room. On that-- I could write a whole book on the furnished room that we  had to  go through. And then we were so desperate. I didn't have nothing to my sister or my brother. But I sent them a picture, thinking they're going to like it. And I look so sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: They say, what's the matter with you? Are you all right? Do you have-- are you eating? And then is when she told the lady who sponsored us-- she always wanted to send them packages.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, we don't need. But my sister in England, she needs. So she sent me. And she's the one who sponsored us all. She sponsored my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sponsored you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her son--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to come to America, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her son sponsored my brother-- her son because my, my brother's friend, he also was in the concentration camp. He was her nephew. And he came here. So the nephew say, help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he brought my brother here first. Then they brought my sister. And then they, they-- her brother-- her son sponsored me and my-- and my husband. I, I wanted to ask you also--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're at--

speaker: interviewer
text: --do you think--

speaker: interviewer
text: --29 here, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. Real quick, then. Do you think it could happen again, the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holocaust? I don't know. It could happen, but I don't think so now that everybody's aware. Do you think so?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know. It could happen because there's such-- everybody-- nothing has changed, really, the minds of people. Some people, they still think it  didn't happen. Don't they-- don't they say so? It didn't happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Or there are so many. It depends how many people are-- who could calm us. Or maybe there are more good people that, that would-- don't let it happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know if it could happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I wouldn't know. I hope it won't happen again. And-- but it's, it's sad that they are allowing these people to talk on TV and tell them about the Jews, like they say all these horrible things, you know, that, that the Jews, the Jews are always against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and this-- I-- but I'm-- I don't think it-- maybe it won't happen. I mean, they learned a lesson, I hope by now. [LAUGHS] You know? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're actually-- I lied. We're about at-- we're 28 now. We have another couple minutes if you--

speaker: interviewer
text: We have another couple of minutes. Well, do you want to switch to the next tape and do the pictures and the-- and the family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you have any final message that you want to say? Any final lesson or message you want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I want to say-- it's a terrible thing, hate. People should love each other, I say. Because if we have-- we have too much hate in this world. It's not fair. It's not right. I say, we are all humans. And we're all the same. We're people. So if they-- that people should think twice before they do something so horrible to other people. But as I say, Hitler, Hitler-- people like Hitler will never learn. But we hope that other people will stop them before it's too late. You know, because here they should have stopped them then. But they didn't. But now they should-- that's a lesson to learn. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.
